item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While I felt this could have been expanded on a little - either investigating the original cause of the anomaly or more testing of the limitations of the space, I found the overall concept of the SCP to be good and the interaction believable, both from the D-class and the Foundation sides. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It seems like many people did not like this one. I, for one, think it's a really interesting and well executed idea and was genuinely sorry they "took it away from them".

Upvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't think it requires you to think that the Foundation is unnecessarily cruel to D-class personnel, but it's basically in their job description that they're the ones who do all the stuff that could get you killed and are the first ones who are to come in contact with an object so that if it IS lethal, valuable researchers and technicians aren't lost in the process.

They're disposable in the same way as, say, a Noodle & Company bowl is. Yes, it's inexpensive and designed to be discarded, but you're much better off using it as food storage for leftovers than to just get rid of it after one use. Likewise, a D-class isn't a huge loss (what with being a murderer most likely) but is better off surviving as long as possible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Show opinion post
- Hide click HERE to close

I really find this kind of weak, echoing other commenters here. It starts pretty strong, but as soon as I got halfway through I realized where it was going.

It REALLY would do much much much better as a Tale, exploring the D-Class culture and them secretly telling new recruits about it. I really really would love to see that tale. Please consider writing that, it’d be wonderful to see.

As this is, it’s not my favourite. But a good effort.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's a fine line between "this meets the spirit of the contest and it's good because of it" and "this meets the spirit of the contest and feels contrived because of it". For me, this falls more into the latter category. The anomaly exists for no real reason, as though it was designed specifically to be a Room What Generates Reader Feels, and that was that. This also relies on traditional, somewhat nonsensical ideas about the Foundation being needlessly cruel to its human testing subjects. Even inmates in American prisons get books, TV and movies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Says that it only works for D-class- drones, Foundation personnel, etc. don't get sent through.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I kinda liked this untill the end of the interwiew, which felt kind of melodramatic to me. Also, what's up with the D-class-in-restroom-stall thing? Cuoldn't they send a drone to investigate.

Novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really liked how it was implied the D-Class involved weren't involved in the particularly awful stuff. I enjoy the thought of there being D-Class doing really mundane boring tests on things that aren't monsters that want to eat them in nasty ways. The interview was also well done, and the last line was a nice coup de grâce on the feels.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is pretty nice, and it's kinda dickish what they did. It coulda been more poignant, but it's nice, in a Series 1 sort of way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Like the article I read two prior to this, it's too much like the "D-Class pretend to be contagious to get a cushy life" skip. In this case, it's more or less the exact same thing, just applicable to more than one person at a time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the scip is pretty well done, but a little hint as to how it came to be would make it that much better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel sorry for the Ds who get to hear about this, but didn't get to go before it was shut down.

Well, *sorrier*. Ds usually get my sympathy on general principle.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh, this one doesn't do much for me, I'm afraid. While there is charm in a D-class "room of requirements", as MrWrong put it, that charm is not really enough to make a full article out of. Perhaps this would have worked as a tale, but as an article it just left me a little underwhelmed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed. While it's not bad, and I think it's worth a mainlist slot, this doesn't really go that far into the Foundation's apparent lack of respect for human life. There aren't any extremes; some guys have a crappy mundane life, they have a good thing going, the foundation takes it away cuz they're a dick. More could be done with this… explore the D-Class that made the place?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Feels weird to say this after my comment on another article, but this is unsatisfying. I guess that one was sort of a moral victory which felt seriously satisfying, whereas the full extent of this one is "D-Class have it hard, here's something to take the edge off". Feels like junk food I guess.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! Although, the D-Class aren't allowed to use it. They did use it before the Foundation knew about it, until D-23588 messed it up for everyone, but now's it's locked up and the D-Class that did know about it aren't there anymore.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I find it quite charming; it's like a D-class Room of Requirement. While I think the site has moved past the "let personnel use SCP" phase, I think an exception can be made for highly-stressed D-class personnel.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My entry for the D-Class Contest. I don't have any expectations about winning, but if this stays up, I'll get to make an author page!

Thanks to Wogglebug for looking at this in the forums.
